It does not stem from a single cause. It is the cumulative result of multiple independent forces — each reasonable in isolation, collectively creating a landscape that is fragmented, ungoverned, and increasingly difficult to manage. Some of the typical ones are as follows:

1) Shadow Automation: Scripts, macros, RPA-based automation that are individual/team/BU managed, undocumented, ungoverned, un-auditable, prone to upgrade instability.

2) Legacy processes: Manual file uploads/extracts via ungoverned interfaces, Direct Database level updates/extracts, Email based data exchange. Each was built to solve a specific problem. Collectively they represent a governance and security gap.

3) Vendor supplied platform or tool, but built for a different era: Legacy VAN based EDI connections. The continued use of such platforms are not aligned with the real-time, API-driven ecosystems that modern partner and supplier relationships demand.

4) Low code/no code tools not purpose-built to serve as an integration layer: platforms designed for departmental productivity, repurposed as connective layer between enterprise systems. They are accessible, fast to deploy, but not designed for the security, governance for enterprise-grade integration needs.

5) Integration platform fragmentation:  When you solve the point-to-point problem by adding more integration tools, resulting in creation of platform-to-platform silos.

This fifth dimension — integration platform fragmentation — deserves a closer examination.

For much of the last two decades, the market and industry narrative positioned SAP's integration platform as a tool for connecting SAP systems to the outside world. The legacy of SAP PI/PO — associated with IDoc and RFC/BAPI mediation for ECC and S/4HANA — created an impression that if your integration scenario did not involve an SAP application at one end, you needed a different platform. That perception was historically understandable. It is now definitively outdated. The historical perception, however, had consequences. It drove organizations toward platform fragmentation — not always through shadow IT decisions, but through considered choices as well.

Some fragmentation followed specific classes of use-cases based platform choice: An API gateway or a file transfer solution, catering to specific business processes, or a message broker managed by plant operations requiring higher autonomy.

Some fragmentation followed the relentless pace of SaaS adoption due to the accelerated digital transformation in the last decade. Each new best-in-class platform adopted for a specific function required connections to existing systems. Each connection, built in isolation, added another node to a landscape that was becoming increasingly difficult to see, manage, and secure as a whole. Had a single, open, enterprise-grade integration platform been the established foundation, much of this complexity would have been absorbed rather than accumulated.

Some fragmentation followed acquisition — an EDI platform inherited with a business unit.

In some cases, a platform was adopted by individual departments moving faster than central IT could govern.

Each was a reasonable decision in its moment. However, the cost of operating multiple integration platforms runs far deeper than a licensing budget spread across vendors. It is the absence of a unified security model — authentication policies, certificate management configured separately on every platform, audited separately. It is the absence of unified operational monitoring — when a business process fails end-to-end, IT sees disconnected system alerts across multiple dashboards while the business sees a failed outcome. It is the talent fragility of institutional knowledge distributed across multiple platform specialisms, where every departure takes irreplaceable context with it. It is the organizational friction of negotiating every new integration requirement across multiple platform owners and governance processes — slowing the enterprise precisely when speed matters most.

And above all of this sits a question every enterprise leadership team is now asking: "are we AI-ready?" The answer depends less on which AI tools or models you have licensed and more on whether your data flows reliably, in real time, across your entire application landscape. A fragmented integration platform cannot provide that foundation. It can only provide fragmented data, at fragmented speed, with fragmented accountability. Integration debt is not just an IT cost. It is the invisible barrier between your enterprise and its AI ambitions.

One comprehensive, versatile, enterprise-grade integration platform — governed, secure, and capable enough to handle every connectivity need in your enterprise is the foundation of a mature digital architecture.


The Breadth: Every System, Every Protocol, Every Pattern

The problem section described an integration landscape shaped by accumulation of ungoverned tools, fragmented platforms, and decisions made without a unified foundation. The answer is not a better point-to-point tool. It is a platform with the breadth to make fragmentation unnecessary in the first place.

SAP Integration Suite is that platform — open, enterprise-grade, and designed to connect every application, process, and data in your landscape, regardless of vendor, protocol, or deployment model. Its breadth is best understood not as a list of adapters, but as coverage across every integration pattern and every layer of the modern enterprise architecture.

1. API-Led Connectivity — The Language of the Modern Enterprise

REST, OData, SOAP, HTTP — Integration Suite speaks the full API vocabulary of the modern enterprise. Whether you are connecting cloud-native SaaS platforms, exposing on-prem resources as managed APIs, or consuming partner and ecosystem APIs, Integration Suite handles every synchronous and asynchronous API interaction pattern out-of-the-box.

2. Event-Driven Architecture — Real-Time, at Enterprise Scale

The emergence of event-driven architecture is one of the defining paradigms in enterprise integration technology today. Business processes no longer wait for scheduled batch runs or manual triggers — they respond to events as they happen, across systems, in real time.

SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh is SAP's flagship offering to support the event driven journey of enterprises. It is an enterprise-grade event streaming and management platform that enables real-time, event-driven communication across distributed systems and applications. It complements traditional integration patterns like API-led integration. The platform's advanced features, such as dynamic message routing, message replay enable organizations to achieve seamless, real-time business processes.

Beyond Advanced Event Mesh, Integration Suite enables connectivity to the full ecosystem of enterprise messaging infrastructure: Apache Kafka, IBM MQ, RabbitMQ, Apache ActiveMQ, Azure Service Bus, Google Pub/Sub, Amazon Kinesis, and Amazon EventBridge — via purpose-built native adapters. Whatever messaging infrastructure your enterprise already runs, Integration Suite connects to it seamlessly.

3. File, Batch, and Legacy Exchange — The Unglamorous Workhorses That Still Run Your Business

Not every integration is real-time. Not every system speaks the API language. A significant portion of enterprise data exchange still moves through file-based and batch processes — and will for the foreseeable future.

Integration Suite supports SFTP, SMB, and mail-based exchange protocols natively, alongside AS2 and AS4 for secure, standards-compliant B2B document exchange. AS2 and AS4 are the modern, encrypted, auditable replacement for the legacy VAN-based EDI connections — giving you a governed, standards-based path to modernize B2B connectivity without disrupting partner relationships.

4. Database Connectivity — Reaching the Data Where It Lives

Not every system exposes a clean API. At times, platforms require direct database-level connectivity to participate in business process integration. Integration Suite connects natively to a range of databases via DB specific adapters: Oracle (cloud and on-premise), Microsoft SQL Server (cloud and on-premise), PostgreSQL, IBM DB2, SAP HANA (cloud and on-premise), MongoDB, Snowflake, Google BigQuery, Amazon DynamoDB, and Azure CosmosDB.

5. SaaS Connectivity — application specific connectors

At first glance, connecting to a SaaS application seems simple: make an HTTP call to its REST API. In reality, that call is only the beginning. Each application has its own authentication mechanisms, pagination rules, rate limits, error formats, and evolving data models. Every new SaaS platform requires developers to learn those nuances, build custom logic around them, and maintain that logic as APIs change over time. What appears simple at the surface quickly becomes a growing layer of hidden complexity.

SAP Integration Suite removes that burden through purpose-built SaaS adapters. These are not generic HTTP connectors. They are pre-configured and pre-tested adapters that understand the specific behaviours of each platform — handling authentication, data structures, and platform-specific constraints out of the box.

The result is not just faster implementation. It is reduced long-term maintenance, lower risk during API changes, and a standardized integration approach across your SaaS landscape.

The coverage spans the full breadth of the enterprise SaaS landscape: Salesforce and Salesforce Pub/Sub, ServiceNow, Workday, BambooHR, NetSuite, Coupa, Anaplan, HubSpot, Jira, Zendesk, Docusign, Shopify, BigCommerce, SugarCRM, Splunk, Snowflake, Box, Dropbox, Microsoft Dynamics CRM, Microsoft SharePoint, Microsoft OneDrive, Microsoft Teams, Microsoft Outlook, Microsoft OneNote, Slack, MongoDB, RabbitMQ, and many more.

In practice, this means that when a new SaaS platform enters your enterprise, you are not starting from scratch. You are extending a governed foundation — not adding another layer of custom code.

6. Hyperscaler Connectivity — Your bridge to IaaS/PaaS platforms (AWS, Azure, GCP, ...)

Most enterprises today do not operate on a single cloud platform. Their workloads are distributed across multiple providers — shaped by architectural choices, financial considerations, and historical decisions accumulated over time. Each hyperscaler has its own native services, its own messaging infrastructure, its own storage and database layer.

Integration Suite connects natively to all the major platforms: AWS via S3, SQS, SNS, SWF, Kinesis, EventBridge, and DynamoDB adapters; Azure via Service Bus, Cosmos DB, and Storage adapters; Google Cloud via BigQuery, Cloud Storage, and Pub/Sub adapters.

This is not generic HTTP connectivity to cloud endpoints. These are hyperscaler-native adapters that speak in the language of native protocols, authentication models, and service behaviours of each cloud platform. Integration Suite can serve as a governed, unified layer across your multi-cloud infrastructure — the single place where cross-cloud data flows are defined, secured, monitored, and managed.

In effect, SAP Integration Suite is your cross-cloud integration fabric.

7. AI Connectivity — The Integration Backbone for the Intelligent Enterprise

As enterprises move from AI experimentation to AI operationalization, AI itself becomes a powerful capability to be integrated into core business processes that span multiple applications. Integration Suite's dedicated AI Adapter connects natively to SAP AI Core's Generative AI Hub and to third-party AI providers — making it the governed integration layer through which AI enters your business processes.

8. Open Connectors — 170+ Additional Applications, One Normalized Layer

For applications not covered by purpose-built adapters, Open Connectors provides normalized, virtualized connectivity to over 170 additional non-SAP cloud applications through a unified adapter layer — with consistent authentication handling, normalized data models, and a single development experience regardless of the underlying application.

9. Pre-Built Integration Content — Accelerating Time to Value

Connectivity is necessary but not sufficient. What enterprises also need is the ability to move fast — to implement integrations without starting from a blank canvas every time. Integration Suite's pre-built integration content — available through the SAP Business Accelerator Hub — provides a library of ready-to-use integration flows, APIs , events, and much more covering both SAP and non-SAP scenarios. Rather than building integration logic from scratch, teams start from a SAP-delivered baseline and configure rather than code. This is what out-of-the-box integration capability means in practice: faster implementation, lower technical debt, and standardized patterns that are easier to maintain, upgrade, and govern over time.

What This Breadth Means in Practice

Virtually no system in your enterprise landscape — legacy or modern, on-premise or private cloud or public cloud, SAP or non-SAP — is outside the reach of Integration Suite. When a business unit adopts a new platform, there is almost certainly a supported, governed path to integrate it.

SAP Integration Suite is the best positioned platform on which the integration debt problem — in all five forms described earlier — finds its answer.


The Depth: Security, Governance, Resilience

Connectivity establishes reach. Security, governance, and resilience establish trust. Here is how Integration Suite earns it.

1. Security

Integration Suite secures the full perimeter of your integration landscape — from user access and credentials, to securing the message payloads, to backend connectivity, to your externally exposed APIs.

  • Access & Identity Control: Integration Suite governs access at multiple levels — not just who can log in, but also what they can do. And at the identity layer, SAP Cloud Identity Services provides centralized identity and access management across SAP BTP — supporting Single Sign-On and identity federation across your enterprise landscape. Role-collection based user management controls who can design, deploy, monitor, and operate integrations across each environment. Access policies feature goes further — defining which specific integration packages and artifacts each team member can access, enabling precise control within large, distributed integration teams. Together this follows the model of Principle of Least Privilege.
  • Credential & Key Management: Integration Suite eliminates the need for placing hardcoded credentials in integration scenarios — one of the most silent and costly vulnerabilities in custom-built integrations — through a range of supported artifacts such as Security material, Keystore and Secure parameter.
  • Message-Level security: Encryption, decryption, digital signing, and verification are available out-of-the-box — ensuring data is protected and verifiable at the payload level, not just at the transport layer.
  • Secure backend connectivity: Cloud Connector helps extend secure, governed connectivity to on-premise systems without opening firewall ports. It supports  fine grain access control of what backend resources to expose via what protocol. Cloud Connector supports Principal Propagation to propagate your end-user context to the backend to ensure role based resource access and auditability.
  • API Security: API Management capability of Integration Suite protects enterprise API assets from OWASP threats through measures such as rate limiting, quota enforcement, and IP allowlisting — with full visibility into API consumption through an enterprise developer portal and API analytics. The recently released AI-based features such as Anomaly detection and API traffic prediction, helps API product owners derive actionable insights.

2. Governance

Integration Suite covers the full governance lifecycle — assessing your integration landscape, standardizing how integrations are designed, controlling how changes move across environments, monitoring operations, and maintaining the audit record. A governance framework is only as effective as the organizational commitment behind it. Integration Suite provides the tools — the discipline to apply them consistently is what transforms a platform investment into a governed integration landscape.

  • Integration Assessment (ISA-M): SAP's Integration Solution Advisory Methodology, now part of Integration Assessment capability provides a structured framework for designing, assessing and enforcing your integration strategy, to take your organization's integration maturity to a higher level. This includes defining the reference domains, styles and use-case patterns — before a single new integration is built. Governance starts here.
  • Design Guidelines & BTP Guidance Framework: Guidelines to Design Enterprise-Grade Integration Flows and BTP Guidance Framework ensure integrations are built to a consistent standard from day one.
  • Transport Management using CTMS: SAP Cloud Transport Management Service (CTMS) is a cloud-native BTP solution to help in robust change management.
  • Operational Visibility with Cloud ALM: SAP Cloud ALM provides the operational control tower for your integration landscape — integration flow monitoring, technical health monitoring (such as JMS resources, certificate expiry), and business process monitoring that correlates integration activity to the business outcomes it supports.
  • Compliance & Audit — SAP Audit Log Service: SAP Audit Log Service maintains an audit record of every significant system change event happening in your tenant.

3. Resilience

  • Integration Suite is built on a proven open-source foundation, with the runtime reliability, message delivery guarantees, and deployment flexibility that business-critical integration demands.
  • The Apache Camel stack: SAP Integration Suite is built on Apache Camel — a proven open-source integration framework.
  • Quality of Service: Integration Suite supports a range of quality of service options, as required by your integration scenarios - be it best effort, guaranteed delivery, and so on.
  • Retry Handling: Standard retry handling for asynchronous scenarios, built into the Pipeline concept, giving you a capability familiar to you if also operate an SAP PI/PO landscape.
  • Structured Error Handling using SAP AIF: SAP Application Interface Framework (AIF) provides a structured, governed error handling for your ERP processes. A powerful feature of AIF is that it empowers business users to monitor interfaces and troubleshoot issues without IT, thereby reducing the time needed for error handling.
  • Infrastructure Resilience with In-metro DR: High availability is ensured through multi-availability zone deployment within a metro region — with defined RTO and RPO commitments, making Integration Suite the viable backbone for your business-critical processes.
  • Deployment Flexibility using Edge Integration Cell: For enterprises with data residency requirements, or processes that must execute on-premise, the Edge Integration Cell provides a on-premise or private cloud deployment option, in the hyperscaler platform of your choice.


The AI-Ready Enterprise Starts Here

Every meaningful AI initiative your organization is pursuing — whether Generative AI based or Agentic AI — shares one non-negotiable prerequisite: connected, governed, real-time data flowing reliably across your entire enterprise landscape.

This is not a future requirement. It is a present one. Enterprises that are moving fastest on AI are not doing so because they have the most advanced models. They are doing so because they resolved their integration foundation first — establishing the governed, real-time data flows that AI depends upon before AI needed them.

SAP Integration Suite is the best positioned platform in your landscape capable of connecting every system, governing every flow, and providing the end-to-end data reliability that AI operationalization demands — regardless of whether those systems are SAP or not.

The enterprises that treat integration modernization and AI readiness as the same investment — made once, on the right platform — will arrive at AI-at-scale ahead of those that treat them as separate workstreams and rebuild the foundation twice. The integration foundation you build today is the AI infrastructure you will depend on tomorrow.

entry.title: The iVolve Series | Your Integration Estate Is Holding You Back. Well, It Doesn’t Have To.
entry.content:
Somewhere in your organisation, there is an integration landscape that is costing you more than it should, moving slower than the business demands, and carrying more risk than anyone has fully quantified. It probably spans more than one middleware platform, perhaps several. It almost certainly lacks complete documentation. And the team maintaining it is spending the majority of its time keeping the lights on rather than delivering anything new.

This is not a niche problem. It is the default state of enterprise integration in 2025. Decades of organic growth, acquisitions, departmental autonomy, and pragmatic decisions made under project pressure have produced landscapes of extraordinary complexity and extraordinary cost. Ask any CIO how many integration platforms are actively running in their organisation and the answer is rarely one. More typically it is three, four, or five: SAP PI/PO handling core SAP connectivity, MuleSoft managing API integrations, a legacy webMethods instance still processing B2B traffic, Boomi picked up through an acquisition, and perhaps a remnant BizTalk deployment that nobody has found the budget to retire.

The Multi-Platform Problem: Why Fragmentation Costs More Than Anyone Realises

Running multiple integration platforms simultaneously is rarely a deliberate strategic choice. It is almost always the accumulated result of individual decisions that each seemed reasonable at the time. But the cumulative effect, technically and commercially is severe, and it compounds with every passing year that the landscape is left in its fragmented state.

The Technical Cost of Fragmentation

Each platform in a multi-middleware landscape brings its own runtime, its own design-time tooling, its own monitoring stack, its own error handling patterns, and its own deployment and release process. The consequence is a set of technical problems that do not appear on any single platform’s licence invoice but are very real in their impact on the integration team and on the business it serves.

  • No single pane of glass : When an end-to-end business process fails, an order that never made it to the warehouse, a payment that did not reach the finance system, the operations team must investigate across multiple monitoring tools, each with different log formats, different retention windows, and different levels of observability. What should be a five-minute investigation becomes a 90-minute cross-platform triage exercise.
  • Incompatible security and governance models : Credential management, access policies, encryption standards, and transport protocols are defined separately on every platform. Applying a new security policy consistently across a fragmented landscape requires parallel effort on every system and creates gaps where inconsistencies go undetected. Audit and compliance exercises that should surface clean evidence instead expose a patchwork of different controls applied to different degrees.
  • Tribal knowledge and platform silos : Deep expertise on any one middleware platform takes years to build. An organisation running four platforms must maintain four pools of specialist knowledge, often held by individuals rather than teams. The departure of one expert can leave entire parts of the integration landscape effectively unsupported. And because interfaces on different platforms were built by different teams using different patterns, cross-platform consistency in design standards, error handling, and documentation is rarely achieved.
  • No coherent high-availability story : Failover, disaster recovery, and planned maintenance management must be solved separately for every platform. Each has different failover mechanisms, different RPO and RTO characteristics, and different operational runbooks. The result is an integration estate where resilience is patchy and inconsistent - high for some interfaces, untested or absent for others.

The Commercial Cost of Fragmentation

The financial consequences of a fragmented integration landscape are rarely visible in a single budget line which is precisely why they persist. They are distributed across licence costs, infrastructure costs, personnel costs, and the operational overhead of managing complexity. When Tarento models the full Total Cost of Ownership for a typical multi-platform landscape, the numbers are consistently eye-opening.

  • Duplicate licence and support costs : Each middleware platform carries its own annual licence, its own vendor support contract, and its own renewal negotiation cycle. Organisations paying MuleSoft licence fees while also holding SAP PI/PO maintenance contracts and already covered for SAP Integration Suite under their RISE or GROW subscription are in the uncomfortable position of paying three times for integration capability they could consolidate onto one platform. Tarento’s ROI Calculator consistently shows annual licence and support savings of hundreds of thousands to millions of pounds once consolidation is modelled against the full current-state cost base.
  • Multiplied infrastructure and operations overhead : On-premise and hybrid middleware platforms require infrastructure to run: servers, patching cycles, DR environments, and the engineering time to maintain them. Cloud platforms require their own tenants, their own environment management, and their own upgrade planning. Multiplied across three, four, or five platforms, the infrastructure and operational overhead is a significant and recurring cost that consolidation can largely eliminate.
  • The hidden cost of slower delivery : New integration requirements, connecting a new SaaS application, enabling a new supplier, automating a new business process must be scoped and delivered separately on whichever platform happens to own that domain. Routing decisions are made not by strategic logic but by which team has capacity. Reusable assets built on one platform cannot be leveraged on another. The net effect is that a fragmented landscape systematically slows down the pace at which the business can evolve its digital operations, with a real but difficult-to-quantify opportunity cost attached to every delayed integration project.

 

The Multi-Platform Tax: Every integration platform an organisation runs above one adds licence cost, infrastructure overhead, operational complexity, and staffing expense. Tarento’s TCO modelling of multi-platform landscapes consistently shows that the annual cost of running three or more middleware platforms, when all cost components are properly accounted for is 2 to 4 times higher than the equivalent cost on a single, consolidated cloud-native platform. This is the multi-platform tax. And most organisations have been paying it for years without fully recognising it.

 

 

Why Leading Enterprises Are Consolidating Now

The multi-platform problem is not new. What has changed is that the pressure to solve it has become acute at exactly the same moment that a credible consolidation target has emerged and the tools to execute the migration have matured to the point where the economics are compelling.

Global enterprise leaders, from Nordic consumer goods conglomerates and global port equipment manufacturers to Asian diversified conglomerates and global specialty chemicals companies, are executing integration consolidation programmes not because it is strategically tidy, but because the business case is undeniable. Reduce the licence estate. Eliminate the infrastructure overhead. Build a single, expert integration team rather than four specialist silos. Adopt a unified monitoring and governance model. And position the integration layer to support the AI-driven business transformation agenda that every board is now being held accountable for delivering.

SAP Integration Suite on BTP has become the consolidation target of choice for SAP-centric enterprises. It is not simply that it is included in RISE and GROW subscriptions, though that matters significantly for the financial case. It is that the platform genuinely delivers the breadth of integration capability that was previously split across multiple tools: A2A, B2B/EDI, API management, event-driven integration through SAP Event Mesh, and pre-built connectivity to thousands of applications through the integration content catalogue. Consolidating onto SAP Integration Suite does not require the business to accept reduced capability. In most cases, it expands it.

The question is no longer whether to consolidate. The question is how to do it without the budget spiralling, the timeline extending indefinitely, and the business being disrupted in the process. That is exactly the question that Tarento has been answering, in production, for some of the world’s most demanding enterprises.

We have migrated landscapes spanning 150 to more than 2,500+ interfaces. We have consolidated platforms ranging from SAP PI/PO and MuleSoft to webMethods, Boomi, BizTalk, Informatica and others -  all onto SAP Integration Suite. And we have done it faster, cheaper, and with lower risk than any purely manual approach could achieve. The reason is iVolve: Tarento’s AI-powered integration modernisation programme, built from the ground up around automation at every stage of the journey.

Today, we are launching a five-part blog series that takes you inside the iVolve approach; from the strategic decisions that set a programme up for success, to the automation tools that make the numbers work, to the operational capabilities that keep a modernised landscape running at enterprise grade. Whether you are a CTO building the case for a board that wants to see ROI, an integration architect scoping a migration you know needs to happen, or an IT operations leader looking for optimisations, this series is written for you.

 

 

The State of Enterprise Integration in 2026

Enterprise integration is at an inflection point. Three forces are converging to make the status quo untenable for most large organisations.

Force 1: The End-of-Support Clock

SAP PI/PO mainstream maintenance ends in 2027. SAP CPI Neo is being deprecated in favour of Cloud Foundry. webMethods is mid-transition following its acquisition by IBM. MuleSoft licensing costs continue to escalate. BizTalk has been in extended support for years. For organisations on any of these platforms, the migration question is not a strategic preference, it is a deadline. Every quarter of inaction is a quarter closer to running unsupported, unlicensed, or unviable software at the heart of business operations.

Force 2: The Economics of Cloud-Native Integration

SAP Integration Suite on BTP is included as part of most SAP RISE and GROW subscriptions. Organisations standardising on S/4HANA, SAP Cloud, or the broader SAP BTP ecosystem are already paying for world-class integration capability, and leaving it unused while continuing to pay separate licences for legacy middleware. The TCO differential, when properly modelled, is striking. Tarento’s customers have seen financial projections showing returns of 200% to 350% over ten years, with break-even periods as short as 12 to 18 months. The economics of staying put are simply no longer defensible.

Force 3: The Velocity Imperative

Business units are no longer willing to wait 12 weeks for a new integration to be delivered by an overwhelmed integration team managing 500 legacy interfaces. The AI-driven business transformation programmes that CEOs are committing to require an integration layer that can move at the pace of the business; real-time, event-driven, API-enabled, and capable of connecting new SaaS applications in days rather than months. Legacy middleware, by design, cannot deliver this. Cloud-native integration platforms, properly implemented and operated, can.

An Emerging Context : AI and Integration

The iPaaS market is experiencing explosive growth, projected to reach $79.38 billion by 2030, driven by AI's transformative impact on integration platforms. AI is revolutionizing iPaaS by enabling intelligent automation, self-healing infrastructure, and proactive monitoring, shifting platforms from reactive data movers to intelligent hubs that predict failures and optimize workflows.

Leading vendors like Boomi, Workato, Informatica, and SAP are rapidly integrating AI-powered assistants and low-code/no-code capabilities to democratize integration development, allowing citizen developers to build automation without deep technical expertise. A critical driver is real-time data access: agentic AI applications demand seamless, context-aware data retrieval across enterprise systems, making AI-powered integration platforms essential for next-generation AI workflows.

However, organizations face emerging security challenges, by 2028, 25% of enterprise breaches may stem from AI agent abuse, making governance and built-in guardrails paramount. The shift from "SaaS with AI features" to "AI-native SaaS" in 2026 signals that platforms embedding AI natively from inception will dominate, while legacy integration approaches become obsolete in supporting modern AI-driven digital transformation.

SAP leads the 2025 Gartner Magic Quadrant for iPaaS (fifth consecutive year) with generative AI capabilities enabling workflow automation, API anomaly detection, and intelligent data mapping. The platform offers 10,500+ pre-built artifacts and 250+ third-party adapters, with 95% customer recommendation rates. SAP positions Integration Suite as the critical infrastructure for agentic AI applications requiring secure, real-time API access across enterprise systems.

However, there continues to be an ongoing debate about SAP's strength primarily being with existing SAP customers seeking to consolidate iPaaS and connect SAP with non-SAP systems, rather than as a standalone iPaaS competitor. Personally, I feel that SAP has closed the gap significantly and is now a serious contender, with an added advantage of having the edge when it comes to operating in a SAP centric ecosystem.

 

Why Migration Programmes Fail, and Why iVolve Is Different

Integration migration projects have a poor reputation for good reason. They routinely overrun timelines, exhaust budgets, and fail to deliver the operational improvements that justified the investment. The pattern is familiar: an optimistic initial estimate that did not account for the full complexity of the landscape, a manual migration process that scales badly, a testing phase that consumes months because test cases had to be written from scratch, and a post-go-live environment that lacks the governance and tooling to be properly operated.

iVolve was purpose-built to break this pattern. Tarento is an SAP Validated Partner Use Case holder and an SAP Migration Factory Partner. iVolve is available on the SAP Store. The iVolve Framework replaces the manual, error-prone, effort-intensive stages of a migration with automation, AI, and proven accelerators achieving automation factors of 30% to 95% depending on the source platform, compressing programme timelines from years to months, and delivering a post-migration landscape that is actively governed, monitored, and resilient from day one.

 

What’s in the Series

Over five blogs, we cover every stage of the integration modernisation journey, the various iVolve tools and accelerators including approaches that make each stage faster, more certain, and more valuable.

 

Part A

The Integration Modernisation Imperative

The strategic case for integration modernisation, why SAP Integration Suite is the right target platform, and how the iVolve lifecycle framework addresses every stage from legacy discovery to operational excellence.

Part B

Making the Case

How to build a compelling, finance-grade business case for SAP Integration Suite consolidation including TCO modelling, NPV, IRR, and the ROI Calculator tool that has helped enterprises from global manufacturers to diversified conglomerates unlock investment approval.

Part C

Automate Everything

A deep dive into the iVolve automation engine; the Discovery Agent, Migration Automator, the MINT App for Neo-to-Cloud Foundry migrations, and the TestEase Bundle with real programme benchmarks showing automation factors of 30% to 95% across every major source platform.

Part D

Beyond Go-Live

How the iVolve OpEase Suite; transport governance, enhanced monitoring, AI-powered alerts, and the iFlow AI Advisory App ensures a modernised SAP Integration Suite landscape operates with the rigour and intelligence that enterprise operations demand.

Part E

When Downtime Is Not an Option

Why organisations that run real-time business operations on SAP Integration Suite need a proven failover strategy, and how the OneFailover Application delivers near-zero downtime through automated sync, orchestrated cutover, and continuous readiness scoring.

 

Who Should Read This Series

These blogs are written for the leaders and practitioners who are closest to the integration challenge, and the ones who will be held accountable for solving it.

  • CTOs and IT leadership who need to make a defensible case for integration modernisation investment and want the financial framework and customer evidence to do it
  • Enterprise and solution architects who are planning a migration, want to understand the automation tooling in depth, and need to scope a programme that will actually deliver on its estimates
  • Integration operations teams who are managing a live SAP Integration Suite landscape and want to understand the tools available to improve resilience, governance, and operational intelligence
  • Finance and procurement leaders who will be asked to approve a migration investment and want to understand the financial model, the risk profile, and the track record behind the proposal

Start Now: If you are running SAP PI/PO, MuleSoft, webMethods, Boomi, BizTalk, or any legacy middleware and are ready to understand what a modern, automated migration to SAP Integration Suite would actually look like for your landscape, start with Blog 1 and 3. If you need the financial case first, go straight to Blog 2. And if you are already on SAP Integration Suite and are wondering whether your landscape is as resilient as it needs to be, Blogs 4 & 5 are for you. 

To explore this further, drop me a note on shabarish.nair@tarento.com

entry.title: iVolve Series | Part A : The Integration Modernization Imperative
entry.content:
Note: This blog is part of The iVolve Series | Your Integration Estate Is Holding You Back. Well, It Doesn’t Have To.

Enterprise integration sits at the heart of every digital transformation. Yet for many organisations, the integration layer built incrementally over decades on platforms like SAP PI/PO, MuleSoft, webMethods, BizTalk, or Boomi has quietly become one of the most significant barriers to change. Hundreds or even thousands of interfaces, fragmented across multiple middleware platforms, maintained by specialists, and carrying the institutional memory of decades of business logic.

The question is no longer whether to modernise and consolidate. It is how to do it without breaking the business in the process.

The Fragmented Integration Landscape: A Universal Problem

Most enterprises did not plan to end up with multiple integration platforms. They grew through acquisitions, departmental autonomy, and vendor-led decisions. The result is a sprawling landscape of technical debt:

  • Multiple middleware platforms each with their own licensing costs, upgrade cycles, and specialist skills requirements
  • Integration logic scattered across hundreds or thousands of discrete artefacts with little or no documentation
  • Aging on-premise infrastructure that creates bottlenecks for cloud adoption and real-time processing
  • Integration teams spending the majority of their time on maintenance rather than value-adding innovation

 

Real-World Context

According to Tarento's iVolve programme data, customers commonly present with 150 to over 2,500+ interfaces requiring modernisation across platforms including SAP PI/PO, MuleSoft, webMethods, Boomi, Informatica, and BizTalk.

 

 

Why SAP Integration Suite Is the Strategic Target

SAP Integration Suite on BTP represents a fundamentally different kind of platform. It is cloud-native, continuously updated, AI-enabled, and deeply integrated with SAP's broader business technology ecosystem. For organisations standardising on SAP S/4HANA or the SAP cloud portfolio, it provides a natural home for integration logic, reducing the dependency surface and aligning the integration layer to the pace of the business.

The platform offers pre-built content, open connectivity, B2B/EDI capabilities, API management, and event-driven integration, all under a single subscription. The operational economics of eliminating separate middleware licences, infrastructure, and specialist teams are substantial.

We have also expanded on the emerging AI context in the previous blog and how SAP is positioning itself as a leading integration platform.

 

The iVolve Approach: Accelerators, AI, and Automation Across the Lifecycle

Tarento's iVolve programme was built around a single insight: migration projects fail or overrun not because of a lack of skilled consultants, but because of the sheer volume of manual, repetitive work involved. Every interface needs to be discovered, assessed, redesigned, migrated, documented, and tested. Do this manually for say 500 interfaces and you have a multi-year programme with significant risk.

iVolve introduces automation and AI at every stage of the journey:

 

Feature

iVolve Capability

Legacy Discovery

Discovery Agent automatically scans and catalogues the source landscape, classifying interfaces by complexity and migration readiness

Platform Strategy

Platform Recommendation Tool and Vector Sprint consulting engagement define the target architecture and phased roadmap

Business Case

ROI Calculator provides financial modelling i.e. TCO, annual savings, NPV, IRR  to anchor the investment decision

Migration

Migration Automator drives 30–80% automation of interface migration depending on source platform, with iVolve AI Agentic Workbench handling complex artefacts

Testing

TestEase Bundle provides automated functional, B2B, and coverage testing, eliminating manual test scripting

DevOps & Ops

OpEase Suite provides transport governance, near-zero downtime failover, and AI-powered monitoring

 

 

The Four Pillars of a Successful Integration Modernisation

Tarento's experience across more than 15 completed modernisation programmes, spanning from 150 to over 2,500+ interfaces  has crystallised a four-pillar delivery methodology:

  • Discover with Confidence : You cannot plan what you cannot see. The Discovery pillar uses the iVolve Discovery Agent to produce a complete inventory of the legacy landscape, including interface complexity scoring and a migration approach document. This document gives customers a genuine Go / No-Go decision point before committing to migration spend.
  • Migrate with Automation : The Migration pillar deploys the iVolve Migration Automator against the source platform, generating migrated iFlows at scale. The iFlow AI Advisory App ensures each flow follows appropriate target patterns; not just a literal lift-and-shift, but a modernisation-aware transformation.
  • Test with Assurance : The Testing pillar applies the TestEase Bundle to validate every migrated interface functionally, with automated B2B testing and test coverage analysis. Customers can choose the level of automated versus manual testing engagement based on their internal preferences and risk appetite.
  • Operate with Resilience : The Integration Operations pillar, powered by the OpEase Suite, ensures the post-go-live landscape is actively governed, monitored, and resilient. The OneFailover application provides near-zero downtime failover for critical landscapes. Enhanced monitoring and transport governance complete the picture.

 

 

The Promise: Faster, Cheaper, Lower Risk

The iVolve approach delivers on three dimensions simultaneously. Programmes complete faster because automation removes the long tail of manual migration effort. They cost less because the automation factor directly reduces person-hours, and iVolve requires no additional licensing beyond the SAP BTP subscription the customer is already committing to. And they carry lower risk because automated migration reduces human error, automated testing catches regressions early, and the Discovery Agent means no nasty surprises mid-programme.

In the next blog in this series, we will examine how to build the financial and strategic case for integration modernisation including the specific ROI metrics and business case components that have helped customers from a leading Nordic consumer goods conglomerate to a global beer manufacturer and a major port equipment and automation company unlock budget and executive approval.

 
Know More & Get Started: Drop me a note on shabarish.nair@tarento.com

entry.title: iVolve Series | Part B : Making the Case (Figuring Out RoI, TCO and Other Metrices)
entry.content:
Note: This blog is a continuation of The iVolve Series | Your Integration Estate Is Holding You Back. Well, It Doesn’t Have To. Read Part A : The Integration Modernization Imperative before continuing.

Every integration modernisation programme starts the same way: a technically compelling argument that struggles to gain traction in the boardroom. IT leaders can describe the fragility of the landscape, the rising cost of maintenance, the risk of falling off support. But without a clear financial narrative and without numbers that connect to budget lines, decisions stall.

This blog examines how organisations can build a rigorous, defensible business case for integration consolidation onto SAP Integration Suite, and how Tarento's ROI Calculator accelerator makes that process faster and more credible.

 

Why Business Cases for Integration Migrations Fail

The typical integration modernisation business case fails for one or more of three reasons: it cannot quantify current costs with confidence, it cannot model future costs credibly, and it cannot answer the timing question, "When does the organisation break even, and what does delay cost every month?".

These are solvable problems. But they require a structured analytical approach, not a back-of-envelope estimate.

 

The Total Cost of Ownership Framework

The starting point for any credible business case is a complete picture of what the current integration landscape actually costs. This is almost always higher than initial estimates because costs are distributed across multiple categories:

  • Software licensing - Middleware platform licences, which for commercial platforms like MuleSoft, webMethods or others can be substantial
  • Infrastructure - On-premise server costs including hardware refresh cycles, data centre, and support contracts
  • Personnel - The integration specialists who maintain the landscape, including contractor rates for niche skills
  • Operations -  Monitoring tooling, incident management, on-call costs, and planned maintenance effort
  • Upgrade and compliance risk - The hidden cost of deferred upgrades and the growing risk premium of running out-of-support software

 

 

 

The ROI Calculator: Tarento's ROI Calculator aggregates all of these cost dimensions into a Total Cost of Ownership model for the current state, then calculates the equivalent TCO for a post-migration SAP Integration Suite landscape.

 

The Financial Metrics That Drive Decisions

The iVolve ROI Calculator generates six financial outputs, each designed to answer a specific question that arises in a capital approval process. Together, they build a complete and progressive argument i.e. from “How much do we save?” to “Why is this the best use of this budget?”

Annual Savings : The Recurring Dividend

Annual Savings is the fundamental measure of ongoing value: how much the organisation stops paying every year once migration is complete. Calculated as Current Platform TCO minus SAP BTP annual cost, it aggregates licence savings, infrastructure consolidation, reduced operational overhead, and freed personnel capacity. In Tarento’s multi-platform reference scenario; for example, consolidating SAP PI/PO, MuleSoft, webMethods, and Boomi onto SAP Integration Suite, this figure reaches $1.06 million per year.

Break-Even Period : When the Investment Pays for Itself

Calculated as Migration Cost ÷ Annual Savings × 12, the break-even period tells decision-makers precisely when the migration investment is fully recovered through accumulated savings. It is the most universally understood capital test.

Here is an example from one of our customers. Against a $710K migration investment and $1.06M in annual savings, break-even falls at 9 months - full recovery of the investment before the end of year one. That single figure tends to resolve boardroom hesitation faster than any other number in the analysis.

3/5/10-Year ROI : The Return on Capital

ROI percentage is calculated as (Cumulative Savings − Migration Cost) ÷ Migration Cost × 100. It expresses the total return on the migration investment at standard planning horizons and is the most intuitive measure for non-finance stakeholders. In the reference scenario, the 3-year ROI reaches 346%, meaning the organisation recovers its full investment and receives more than three times that amount in additional savings within three years of completion.

The 5- and 10-year figures compound this further, underpinning the Value Multiple discussed below.

Net Present Value (NPV) : What the Future Savings Are Worth Today

ROI percentage captures the size of the return but not its timing. A migration that delivers the same total savings over three years is worth more than one that delivers it over ten, because earlier cash flows can be redeployed into the business sooner. NPV corrects for this using the time value of money.

Each year’s savings stream is discounted at 10%, reflecting the opportunity cost of capital, the return the organisation could achieve by deploying the same budget elsewhere, and the migration cost is then subtracted.

A positive NPV confirms that the migration creates genuine economic value even after accounting for the cost of capital. In a reference scenario, the 5-year NPV exceeds $3.3 million: the organisation is that much better off in today’s money terms than if it deferred the decision. NPV is the metric finance committees rely on most heavily for capital project comparison, because it normalises for timing and allows direct, like-for-like comparison between competing investment proposals.

Internal Rate of Return (IRR) : The Comparison Benchmark

IRR is the discount rate at which the NPV of the migration equals zero. In other words, the effective annual return rate the project generates on the capital invested. Finance teams use it to rank projects against each other and against the organisation’s hurdle rate (typically the weighted average cost of capital, commonly 8–12%).

For a migration with a 9-month payback and annual savings exceeding 150% of the migration cost, IRR runs well above 100%. That is a figure that clears any standard corporate hurdle rate and compares favourably against virtually any alternative capital deployment. Placing that number alongside the organisation’s cost of capital is typically the moment a finance committee moves from “interesting” to “approved.”

Value Multiple : The Executive Headline

Calculated as 10-Year Total Savings ÷ Migration Cost, the Value Multiple is the simplest executive summary: for every dollar invested in migration, how many dollars does the organisation receive back over a ten-year horizon?

A Value Multiple of 6x or higher, typical in consolidations from high-cost commercial platforms, means every dollar of migration investment returns six dollars or more over the decade. This is often the number that headlines the financial case.

 

Multi-Platform Consolidation: Where the Real Value Hides

For organisations running multiple integration platforms simultaneously, a common situation for post-merger enterprises or those that have gone through multiple vendor cycles, the business case becomes especially powerful. The ROI Calculator supports multi-platform consolidated analysis, modelling the simultaneous convergence of, for example, SAP PI/PO and MuleSoft or webMethods and Boomi onto SAP Integration Suite.

The weighted cost allocation methodology (60% cost weight, 40% integration count weight) produces a platform breakdown showing individual ROI and savings for each source platform, alongside a consolidated savings figure. Migration phasing can be modelled to sequence platforms based on complexity and dependencies, turning an overwhelming consolidation into a manageable roadmap.

 

Three Stakeholder Perspectives, One Calculator

Different stakeholders need different things from a business case. The iVolve ROI Calculator is designed to address all three simultaneously:

  • C-Suite: Transforms complex platform decisions into clear financial metrics i.e. ROI percentage, value multiple, and the cost of delay
  • IT Leadership: Provides technical validation of platform modernisation with effort quantification - interface counts, migration phases, resource requirements
  • Finance Teams: Delivers transparent cost models suitable for capital expenditure approval, with defensible TCO, TCD and RoI calculations

 

The Opportunity Cost of Delay

One of the most powerful elements of the business case is often the monthly opportunity cost of inaction. If the migration delivers for e.g. €88,000 per month in recurring savings, every month of delay represents €88,000 of avoidable cost. Over a six-month decision cycle, that is over half a million euros of savings foregone.

This reframes the investment decision. The question is not whether the organisation can afford to migrate; it is whether it can afford to wait.

 

Customer Example: Global Retail & Asian Conglomerate: At a global retail company and a major Asian diversified conglomerate, ROI insights were used as a catalyst to unblock stalled transformation conversations. Economic clarity replaced hesitation, enabling confident early directional calls.

 

From Business Case to Approved Programme

The ROI Calculator is the starting point of Tarento's customer engagement, typically deployed in a 2-3 week Business Case and ROI Calculation engagement that precedes the formal Discovery phase. By the time the Discovery Agent has completed its assessment and produced the Migration Approach Document, the financial case is already in the hands of the decision-makers.

Customers receive a Go / No-Go decision point with full financial visibility; the migration cost, the annual savings, the break-even date, and the 10-year value. This is the foundation on which integration modernisation programmes are approved and funded.

In the next blog, we examine how to evaluate the RoI of moving and consolidating the enterprise integration landscape on SAP Integration Suite, specifically how iVolve's automation tools deliver on the financial case by slashing the time and cost of discovery, migration, and testing.

 
Know More & Get Started: Drop me a note on shabarish.nair@tarento.com. We can help you evaluate the RoI for your enterprise landscape, and if applicable develop the business case for an integration consolidation.

entry.title: iVolve Series | Part C : Automate Everything
entry.content:
Note: This blog is part of The iVolve Series | Your Integration Estate Is Holding You Back. Well, It Doesn’t Have To. Read iVolve Series | Part B : Making the Case before continuing.

The business case has been approved. The ROI model has convinced the finance committee. Now comes the hard part: delivering on the numbers. Traditional integration migration projects have a poor record here. Timelines slip, effort estimates prove optimistic, and the promised savings evaporate into cost overruns. iVolve was built specifically to close this gap; by replacing manual, error-prone processes with automation at every stage.

 

The Automation Imperative

Consider what a manual migration of 500 interfaces looks like. Each interface must be analysed, documented, recreated on the target platform, adapted for target patterns, tested, and signed off. Even at a generous pace of two interfaces per consultant per day, that is 250 person-days of migration effort alone before testing, before documentation, before rework.

iVolve's Migration Automator changes this equation fundamentally. The migration automator is a multi platform integration consolidation solution. 

While SAP PI/PO migrations remains the main usecase for most customers, the iVolve Migration Automator is built as a true multi-source consolidation engine. Organisations running MuleSoft, webMethods, Boomi, BizTalk, Workato or Informatica iPaaS can feed their existing landscape directly into the same Automator pipeline, with platform-specific extractors and transformation templates handling the proprietary artefact formats, adapter configurations, and mapping constructs of each source. 

The DSL architecture (detailed in the following sections) is what makes the Automator the consolidation tool for heterogeneous landscapes: A single integration modernisation programme can, say for example, simultaneously retire MuleSoft licences, decommission an on-premise webMethods estate, and migrate inherited Boomi integrations from an acquisition, along with the existing SAP PO integration landscape all converging onto one strategic target platform.

The result is not just a faster migration but is the technical foundation that makes enterprise-wide integration consolidation commercially viable at scale.

 Figure: iVolve-supported source platforms and the Automated Discovery & Migration path to SAP BTP Integration Suite

The automation factor, the proportion of interface migration work handled without manual intervention ranges from 30% to 80% depending on source platform:

 

Feature

iVolve Capability

SAP PI/PO → SAP IS

70–80% automation : The highest factor, reflecting deep platform knowledge

MuleSoft → SAP IS

55–65% automation : Covering the majority of standard integration patterns

Boomi → SAP IS

40–50% automation : Strong coverage of common Boomi artefact types

webMethods → SAP IS

30–40% automation : Effective for A2A and B2B/EDI patterns

BizTalk → SAP IS

30–40% automation : With additional manual handling for complex orchestrations

Workato → SAP IS

50–60% automation : Strong coverage of recipe-based flows and standard connector patterns; complex event triggers, lookup tables and custom connectors addressed by the iVolve AI Agentic Workbench (Initial estimate)

Informatica iPaaS → SAP IS

30–40% automation : Effective for common mapping and ETL-style flows; complex PowerCenter and IICS transformation objects handled by the iVolve AI Agentic Workbench (Beta release, initial estimate)

Neo → Cloud Foundry

Up to 95% automation via the MINT App : Near-complete automation for CPI migration

These automation factors translate directly into programme cost savings. A 70% automation factor on a 500-interface SAP PI/PO migration means the equivalent manual effort is reduced to that of a 150-interface programme. The remainder of the migration effort is generated by the automator, with consultants focusing on review, customisation, and edge cases rather than repetitive conversion work.

 

Why SAP Integration Suite, and Why Migration Is Harder Than It Looks

Before examining how iVolve automates the migration journey, it is worth understanding why SAP Integration Suite has become the consolidation target of choice for large enterprises — and why migrating to it from platforms like MuleSoft, Boomi, or webMethods is technically demanding enough to require a purpose-built automation approach.

The Case for SAP Integration Suite

SAP Integration Suite is not merely a like-for-like replacement for legacy middleware. For enterprises running SAP S/4HANA, SAP ECC, or the broader SAP cloud portfolio, it is the native integration platform: pre-wired, pre-certified, and continuously maintained for SAP-to-SAP and SAP-to-third-party connectivity in a way that no third-party middleware can replicate. Its integration content catalogue ships with thousands of pre-built, SAP-certified integration flows for common business scenarios like order-to-cash, procure-to-pay, hire-to-retire etc that organisations on MuleSoft or Boomi must build and maintain from scratch.

Beyond SAP-native connectivity, SAP Integration Suite delivers a genuinely unified integration capability across A2A, B2B/EDI, API management, event-driven integration through SAP Event Mesh, and open connector frameworks, capabilities that on competing platforms typically require separate licensed products and separate operational teams. For organisations on RISE with SAP or GROW with SAP, Integration Suite is included in the subscription, making the commercial case for consolidation straightforward once the full TCO picture is properly modelled. And as SAP's strategic integration platform, it benefits from continuous investment, a growing pre-built content library, and deep alignment with SAP's AI and BTP roadmap in a way that third-party middleware vendors cannot match for SAP-centric landscapes.

The challenge is that arriving on SAP Integration Suite from a legacy platform is not a straightforward technical exercise. Every source platform has its own design paradigm, its own transformation language, its own adapter model, and its own concept of what an integration flow is. The distance between how MuleSoft, Boomi, Biztalk or webMethods express integration logic and how SAP Integration Suite expects that logic to be structured is significant; and bridging it manually, interface by interface, is where migration programmes traditionally consume the most time and generate the most risk.

The Platform Translation Problem

To understand the scale of the translation problem, consider the specific technical gap between MuleSoft and SAP Integration Suite. MuleSoft uses DataWeave as its transformation language; a powerful, purpose-built expression language for data transformation and mapping that is entirely proprietary to the Anypoint platform. SAP Integration Suite uses Groovy scripts and XSLT for equivalent transformation logic. These are fundamentally different languages with different syntax, different data type handling, and different approaches to mapping construction. A MuleSoft landscape with hundreds of flows may contain thousands of individual DataWeave transformations. Manually rewriting each one into Groovy or XSLT, then validating the output against the original behaviour, is the kind of work that consumes months of specialist effort and generates significant regression risk.

The problem is not unique to MuleSoft. Boomi uses its own Process Property and Map Function model for transformations. webMethods uses XSLT and its proprietary flow service constructs. BizTalk uses XSLT maps with functoids. Each represents a different design vocabulary that must be read, interpreted, and translated into the iFlow palette of SAP Integration Suite - not just syntactically, but semantically, preserving the intended business logic of the original flow in a form that is both functionally correct and architecturally aligned with SAP IS best practices.

 

What Every Source Platform Brings to the Migration Table

MuleSoft: DataWeave transformation language, Anypoint Connectors, flow-based design with Sources/Processors/Sinks, MEL expressions, and proprietary error handling scopes

Boomi: Process canvas with Shapes (connectors, maps, decision, notify), Map Functions for transformations, Process Properties for parameter management, and connector-specific operation configurations

webMethods: Flow Services with proprietary step types (INVOKE, BRANCH, LOOP, MAP), IS Documents as data structures, TN (Trading Networks) for B2B, and pipeline-based data flow

BizTalk: Orchestration using .NET-compiled Orchestration Designer, XSLT maps with functoids, Receive/Send Ports with adapter configurations, and MessageBox-based routing

 

How iVolve Solves the Translation Problem: One Schema, Any Source

The architectural breakthrough at the heart of iVolve’s migration capability is the iVolve DSL (Domain Specific Language), a universal canonical format into which any source platform’s integration logic is normalised before translation to SAP Integration Suite. Rather than building a direct, bespoke converter for each source-to-target combination, iVolve’s Extractor Layer reads the source platform’s artefacts - flow definitions, transformation scripts, adapter configurations, routing rules, error handling constructs, and represents them in a single, well-defined intermediate schema.

The Transformation Layer then converts this canonical representation into SAP Integration Suite artefacts: iFlows, Groovy scripts, XSLT mappings, Message Mappings, Value Mappings, and Script Collections using the iVolve Integration Template Definitions that encode SAP IS best practices directly into the code generation logic. The result is not just a functional migration. It is a migration that produces SAP IS iFlows structured the way SAP expects and recommends, with appropriate error handling, logging, externalised parameters, and flow patterns applied consistently across every migrated interface.

Critically, iVolve AI is integrated throughout this process. The AI layer increases accuracy in identifying the most appropriate SAP IS flow components and patterns for each source construct at runtime, handling cases where a direct structural mapping does not exist and intelligent interpretation of intent is required. This is the distinction between mechanical translation and intelligent migration: iVolve does not just convert syntax, it interprets the purpose of each source component and selects the best SAP IS equivalent from the iFlow palette.

From DataWeave to Groovy: Practical Example of Intelligent Code Conversion in Practice

The DataWeave-to-Groovy conversion is one of the most technically demanding aspects of a MuleSoft migration and one of the clearest demonstrations of what iVolve’s automation engine can do. DataWeave is a richly expressive, functional transformation language. A typical DataWeave script for a JSON-to-XML order transformation might combine pattern matching, conditional logic, array operations, date formatting, namespace declarations, and custom function definitions — all in DataWeave’s own idiomatic style.

iVolve’s AI-Native Integration DSL captures the semantic intent of the DataWeave script, what data is being read, what transformation is being applied, what output structure is being produced, and generates the equivalent Groovy script for SAP Integration Suite. The iVolve AI engine, drawing on its Integration Best Practices Knowledge Graph and SAP ISAM literature, ensures that the generated Groovy code follows SAP IS coding standards: correct use of the Message object, proper body and header handling, appropriate use of the SAP IS Groovy API for accessing exchange properties, and clean exception handling patterns. The output is not raw, unstructured Groovy that happens to replicate the DataWeave logic, it is SAP IS-idiomatic Groovy that an SAP integration developer would recognise as well-written, maintainable code.

The same logic applies to XSLT generation from Boomi Map Functions, to the conversion of webMethods INVOKE steps into SAP IS Receiver Channel calls, and to the translation of BizTalk functoid-based maps into SAP IS Message Mapping artefacts. In each case, iVolve reads the source construct, interprets its intent through the iVolve DSL canonical representation, and generates the most appropriate SAP IS equivalent, automatically, at scale, across hundreds of interfaces simultaneously.

Component-by-Component: How Every Legacy Element Finds Its SAP IS Home

iVolve’s component mapping is not a flat one-to-one lookup. It is an intelligent, context-aware interpretation that considers the source component’s function, its position in the flow, its relationship to adjacent components, and the overall integration pattern being expressed. The Migration Automator’s Integration Pattern Parser reads the source flow architecture and maps it to the most appropriate SAP IS palette construct, drawing on iVolve’s library of SAP IS design patterns and best-practice templates.

Some illustrative examples of how this works in practice:

Best-Practice Alignment Built Into the Output

What distinguishes iVolve’s output from a mechanical conversion tool is that SAP Integration Suite best practices are baked into the code generation templates themselves. The iFlow AI Advisory App,  informed by SAP’s Integration Architecture and Methodology (ISAM) literature and Tarento’s own Integration Best Practices Knowledge Graph, guides the choice of target iFlow pattern for each migrated interface before the automator generates the artefact. This means that every iFlow produced by iVolve reflects the correct SAP IS approach, not just a working translation. The application also accelerates the design phase, by guiding integration architects and developers on the right integration pattern of choice.

In practice, this means: adapter configurations are externalised as iFlow parameters from the outset, enabling environment portability without manual rework. Error handling is implemented using SAP IS exception subprocesses with structured logging, rather than embedded try-catch constructs that vary between developers. Message content is manipulated through the correct SAP IS step types; Content Modifier, Filter, Converter - rather than overloaded Groovy scripts that perform multiple functions. Adapters are selected based on the best-practice connectivity pattern for the target endpoint, not simply the closest structural equivalent to the source. And where the source platform used a proprietary pattern that has no direct SAP IS equivalent, such as MuleSoft’s Pub/Sub messaging or Boomi’s low-latency listener model, iVolve applies the appropriate SAP Event Mesh or queue-based pattern from the SAP IS integration content catalogue.

For the integration teams who will maintain the resulting landscape, this matters enormously. A migration that produces functionally correct but architecturally inconsistent iFlows creates a different kind of technical debt, one that will cost time and effort to address in operations. iVolve’s approach ensures that the migrated landscape does not simply replicate the functional behaviour of the original; it lands on SAP Integration Suite as a well-structured, consistently governed, maintainable integration estate that the organisation can run, extend, and evolve with confidence.

 

How to Approach a Modernisation Programme with iVolve? 

Phase 1: Discovery Agent : See Everything Before You Commit

Migration programmes that run into trouble almost always share a common root cause: the source landscape was not fully understood at the outset. Interfaces that appeared simple in the inventory turn out to contain complex Java mappings or custom adapter modules. Dependencies between flows are not documented. Artefacts that were believed to be in use have been dormant for years.

The iVolve Discovery Agent eliminates these surprises. It connects to the source middleware platform like SAP PI/PO, MuleSoft, webMethods, including several others and systematically:

  • Inventories all integration artefacts with full metadata capture
  • Assigns a complexity score to each interface based on mapping complexity, adapter types, and dependencies
  • Identifies artefacts that are unused or candidates for decommissioning rather than migration i.e. a significant cost reduction opportunity
  • Produces a Migration Approach Document that serves as the programme blueprint, including migration plan, effort estimates, and risk assessment
  • Interface recommendations to leverage equivalent SAP Standard integration content, especially for SAP Cloud Solutions.
Accelerate Your Discovery: Tarento has completed discovery engagements across landscapes ranging from 1,000 to 3,500+ interfaces, across SAP PO, Informatica, and BizTalk platforms, each producing a Migration Approach Document within 1-2 weeks.

Phase 2: Migration Automator : iFlows at Scale

With the Discovery complete and the approach document signed off, the Migration Automator is deployed against the source landscape. The automator reads the source artefacts and generates target iFlows for SAP Integration Suite, applying:

  • Appropriate iFlow patterns recommended by the iFlow AI Advisory App, not just a literal copy of source logic but a modernisation-aware transformation
  • Custom logging and error handling frameworks consistent with target platform standards
  • Dynamic Adapter modifications during migration based on target architecture requirements
  • Split functionality to enable Pub-Sub models and add queuing where the target architecture calls for it
  • Reusable integration flow templates to ensure consistency across the migrated landscape

For complex artefacts that the automator cannot handle natively i.e. Java mappings, custom adapter modules, unusual database connections, the iVolve AI Agentic Workbench provides targeted AI-assisted transformation, producing target-specific artefacts or API scaffolds. This aids in further reduction of manual efforts.

The Documentation Agent runs in parallel, generating technical documentation for every iVolve-produced iFlow automatically. This addresses one of the most persistent pain points of legacy integration landscapes: documentation that is absent, outdated, or impenetrable.

Phase 2b: Neo to Cloud Foundry - The MINT App Special Case

For organisations running SAP CPI on the Neo environment, the migration path to SAP Integration Suite on Cloud Foundry (BTP CF) is a distinct technical challenge, and one that iVolve addresses with a purpose-built accelerator: the MINT App (Migration Intelligence Tool). While the main Migration Automator handles cross-platform migrations from legacy middleware, MINT is specifically engineered for the CPI Neo-to-CF transition, and it achieves the highest automation factor in the entire iVolve portfolio: up to 95%.

This matters because the Neo environment is being deprecated by SAP, making the move to Cloud Foundry a mandatory, time-sensitive migration for any organisation that built its integration estate on CPI Neo. Without tooling, this migration involves manually recreating every iFlow, reconfiguring adapters and security artefacts, and retesting every interface, a labour-intensive exercise that consumes months of skilled resource. MINT transforms this into a near-automated, zero-noise transition.

What MINT Does: Five Core Capabilities

  • Discovery & Assessment : MINT automatically scans the current CPI Neo environment and builds a complete inventory of iFlows, connections, and dependencies. This provides a clear baseline of what exists today and precisely what needs attention for the migration, reducing the discovery effort by up to 40-50% compared to a manual assessment exercise.
  • Migration Blueprint : MINT produces structured compatibility reports that identify which artefacts are ready for direct migration to BTP CF and which require adjustment. This blueprint eliminates guesswork from the programme plan and ensures that effort estimates are grounded in a genuine analysis of the landscape rather than informed assumptions.
  • One-Click Migration Accelerators : This is where MINT’s 95% automation factor is realised. Built-in migration accelerators automate the conversion of iFlows and configurations that would otherwise require days of manual rework per interface. The migration effort that would traditionally occupy a team of consultants for months is compressed into a structured, automated execution, drastically cutting project timelines and resource requirements while preserving integration logic fidelity.
  • Event Mesh Enablement : MINT does not just replicate the Neo landscape on Cloud Foundry, it opens the door to future-ready architecture. Migration paths supported by MINT include enablement for SAP Event Mesh and event-driven integration models, allowing organisations to modernise not just their runtime environment but their integration architecture patterns, moving from point-to-point towards scalable, event-driven designs that are responsive to real-time business events across the enterprise.
  • Progress Tracking Dashboard : A visual dashboard provides end-to-end visibility into migration progress, blockers, and success metrics throughout the programme. Stakeholders and programme managers can track milestones and monitor completion rates in real time, ensuring the project stays on schedule with complete transparency, giving leadership confidence at every stage of the transition.

What the Numbers Say

MINT delivers up to 95% automation for Neo-to-Cloud Foundry migrations, the highest automation factor across the entire iVolve portfolio. Discovery effort is reduced by 40-50%, migration effort by up to 75%, and post-migration operational costs by up to 30%. The result is a zero-noise transition: interfaces move, the business keeps running, and the integration team emerges with a modern, cloud-native landscape on SAP BTP Integration Suite.

 

With the Neo-to-CF migration complete and the wider landscape modernisation underway, the next critical phase is ensuring that every migrated interface works exactly as intended. That is the job of the TestEase Bundle.

Phase 3: TestEase : Automated Testing as a First-Class Citizen

Testing is where migration projects historically consume disproportionate time and cost. Creating test cases, preparing test payloads, executing tests, analysing results, and retesting after fixes; all of this was manual, slow, and dependent on business involvement at inconvenient times.

 The TestEase Bundle automates this across multiple testing dimensions:

  • Functional Testing : TestEase automatically generates test suites, test cases, and payloads from SAP business documents. End-to-end message flow validation traces both technical and functional acknowledgments. Tests are reusable, payloads are captured at each lifecycle event and can be replayed for regression testing.
  • B2B / EDI Testing : Trading partner configuration testing validates sender and receiver ID mapping, message types, document standards, and partner-specific transformations. Real-time B2B execution monitoring provides step-by-step traceability for every event and transformation.
  • Test Coverage Analysis : The TestCoverage feature within TestEase provides enterprise-grade test coverage analysis with intelligent path detection and visualisation, giving teams confidence that the migrated landscape has been comprehensively validated, not just spot-checked.
